content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Recommend me movies with genuinely unique concepts

**Score:** 117
**Body:** I'm looking for movies where the concept itself is original not just a plot twist or great acting. The kind of film that makes you think, "How did someone even come up with this?"

Any language, any country, any genre is welcome. Please avoid the obvious mainstream picks if possible. Looking for hidden gems too.

EDIT- Wow, I didn’t expect so many comments. Thanks everyone for the movie suggestions. I really appreciate it. I’m slowly going through them one by one.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Being John Malkovich
2. Check out Brick. It's a neo-noir detective story set in an American High School. Cool concept (great acting too) and unique dialogue.
  - Omg!!!! Brick mention! The main character's personality/looks (Brendon) is also said to be influenced by Spike Spiegel from Cowboy Bebop
3. Annihilation 

Zardoz

The Holy Mountain 

Mad God 

Videodrome

Even if you're not on hallucinogens, you'll think you are.
4. Hundreds of Beavers. Man vs Hundreds of beavers, like a life action loony tunes cartoon. 
5. Swiss army man (2016)
6. Playtime by Tati 
7. Marcel The Shell With Shoes On
8. Memento 
9. A Scanner Darkly has really unique animation and a super stacked cast

## 2. 34 years later, A Few Good Men is still the absolute gold standard for legal thrillers. ⚖️🎬

**Score:** 52
**Body:** &#x200B;

I just watched Rob Reiner's 1992 masterpiece A Few Good Men, (recommended by A FEW GOOD MEN on reddit😜) and it blew me away all over again. In an era where modern thrillers rely so heavily on green screens, massive explosions, and frantic editing, this movie is a phenomenal reminder that pure dialogue can be more high-stakes than any action sequence.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. That Nicholson courtroom scene is one of the best single scenes in American cinema. 
  - YOU'RE GODDAMN RIGHT IT IS! 
2. I like that there is no forced romantic side plot. So rare in movies. 
  - Yeah, especially with Demi in it; in those days she was one of the Hollywood "hotties" that everyone wanted as the sexy love interest in their films.
3. My Cousin Vinnie.

Sure, it's a comedy, but it's the best courtroom story (with jokes) ever made.
4. *I deride your truth handling abilities!*
  - No truth handler, you!
5. Judgement At Nurmeberg is my fav courtroom movie 

Def recommend it
6. I just watched Primal Fear with the wife last week. I had seen it many times before. But it was her first viewing. And that movie is intense! Courtrooms. Scams. I also love A Few Good Men
7. I would make the case for The Firm or maybe The Client, but I won't fight you on it.

## 3. Recommend me movies about making movies

**Score:** 42
**Body:** Idk why I just love that premise so much. Whether it's a tribute to Old Hollywood or a drama/comedy focusing on the movie making itself, I love them all. So far I've seen and loved:

Hail, Caesar!

Babylon 

Once Upon a Time In Hollywood 

The Studio (TV show)

Any other recs? Also non-fiction BTS footage could work too

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. American Movie

Ed Wood


  - I came here to suggest American Movie. It's so good.
2. *Living in Oblivion* (1995), for that indie feel
3. The Stunt Man (1980) a fantastic movie starring Peter O’Toole and Barbara Hershey.  
4. Adaptation

Tropic Thunder
5. If you don’t mind a documentary, Hearts of Darkness 
6. The Player
7. Shadow Of The Vampire 🦇
8. Definitely Singin’ In The Rain
9. Bowfinger, The Player, What Just Happened, One Cut of the Dead, Minions & Monsters

## 4. Civil War (2024)

**Score:** 29
**Body:** Since we're coming up on America's 250th birthday, I am recommending this dystopian vision and cautionary tale of a modern American civil war.  This is from the same director that gave us *Ex Machina* and *28 Days Later*, so expect the violence and social commentary that he is known for.  This film stands as one potential answer to the question: What happens if an authoritarian administration refuses to give up power at the end of two terms?

The answer: Chaos.  It is told through the lens of a group of war photographers in the final days of the war.

Personally, I found the film to be very good, but somewhat difficult to watch.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Jesse Plemons brief appearance in that movie is terrifying. Read somewhere that he wasn’t originally supposed to be in it. He filled in as a favor to his wife when the original guy couldn’t do it. Don’t know how true that is
  - heard same thing 
2. I enjoyed it but it really isn't a movie about a modern American civil war. That was just the backdrop.
  - Yeah, I felt it was more a study of human nature in wartime and a tribute to combat journalists. The fact that it was marketed as something more relevant to our current polarized political landscape and released right before the US  presidential election didn’t do it any favors. Outside of that context I think it’s a very strong movie. 
3. The lack of backstory felt intentional to me. These journalists are just documenting the chaos and they probably have no idea how it all fell apart either. It's like we're dropped into the nightmare alongside them, no context given. People want a civics lesson but horror works better when you can't pinpoint the exact breaking point.
4. It’s an amazing movie but I really think it’s much more about Americans relationship to war via the media than about an actual war in the US or about American politics
5. The scene with Jesse Plemons in that movie is incredible. I’ve watched that scene at least a dozen times. 
6. Here's the thing: it's not really a cautionary tale at all because they don't explain HOW the country devolved into civil war. How allies became enemies because of politcal interpretations. That movie was really about photography in war; it could've been set in Iraq, Afghanistan or anywhere in the world and there wouldn't have been any impact on the plot. A real story about a second civil war would likely need to be a series. Season 1 would be the leadup to the war, Season 2 and first half of 3 would be the actual war itself and then the second half of Season 3 is the aftermath. How do we reintegrate after trying to kill each other for a few years, ya know?
7. Garland wrote 28 Days Later, but Boyle directed it. Garland also directed Annihilation, which imo falls into the interesting failure category but is worth watching, and the miniseries Devs, which is fantastic.
8. It is currently free with ads on the red play button site 

## 5. Perfect Days (2023) A beautiful Japanese film.

**Score:** 28
**Body:** A beautiful Japanese film—perfect for a quiet time at home over a cup of coffee. It centers on Hirayama, a calm and somewhat shy Japanese man who spends his days cleaning toilets and taking photos of trees; there isn't much heavy drama, though hints about his past emerge later on. To me, the film beautifully reflects the idea of ​​doing work that benefits others and being grateful for life. I really enjoyed it—I’d rate it an 8 or 9 out of 10.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Wim Wenders is my favorite Japanese director
2. I loved this film. 
3. I think this is probably my favourite movie. I seem to recommend it nearly every time someone asks for a comfort movie here. One of the only ones I can re-watch again and again. Just the right mix of thoughtfulness and contentment, but not too serious. I was in Tokyo last year and it was so like the movie. I’m even gonna go 9 or 10 out of 10! 🏆
4. The toilet cleaning montages set to Lou Reed had me rethinking my whole morning routine. Found out after that the toilets are real, part of a design project in Shibuya, so now my phone is full of pictures of Japanese public bathrooms. My algorithm thinks I'm planning a very specific kind of trip.
5. I just bought Criterion Collection Blu-ray of this.  Will have to watch it soon.  It looks a little slow, doesn't it? 
  - It’s very calm and there’s not really a plot. Don’t expect excitement, but it’s brilliant! 
  - It's gentle.
6. I loved that movie. ❤️
7. It is contemplative, gentle…wonderful.
8. Though i enjoyed the film  i wouldn't say it's a "Japanese" film. It's a foreigner's outsider view on what they think Japan is film. It works as a character study but for me falls apart as a "slice of Japanese life" film. It's just too...weeby.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. This scene in Obsession instantly reminded me of Passengers. Maybe it's a stretch, but both are rooted in the loss of a woman's agency.

**Score:** 1647
**Body:** In Passengers, Jennifer Lawrence's Aurora Lane is deliberately awakened decades early by Jim Preston for companionship, stripping her of the most important choice of her life. The film later attempts a redemption arc, but at its core, it's about a woman having her agency taken away.

In Obsession, Bear knowingly and deliberately makes choices without Nikki's consent, even when he knows she isn't herself. That makes him a genuinely cowardly and morally corrupt individual.

Are there any other good movies that explore similar themes of a woman's agency being taken away?

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The difference is that in Obsession, Bear's initial wish is ignorant of consequences because he doesn't understand what he is doing.

He has no true belief that the wish is real and it is akin to praying to God that true love will find him.

As the movie goes on however, Bear becomes evil as he ignores the obvious consequences of his actions and tries to justify what he has done by believing that Nikki should be happy. 

In Passengers, the sin is immediate. Jim knows what he is doing is wrong before he does it and does it anyway.

Passengers should have been a horror movie where Jim's actions are only revealed to the audience half way through the movie.

Instead some idiot decided it was romantic...
  - I honestly would love to see a fan edit of Passengers that corrects this.
    - There was a stretch of about 10 years there where lots of 'love stories' were marketed and released that were not in any way love stories. They were popular but they were toxic.

50 Shades of Grey is the most obvious one as that was absolutely not a healthy relationship and it completely and utterly misunderstood what a healthy BDSM relationship looks like.
      - It's been longer than that.  The 80s were great for having toxic relationships (i.e. stalking and violating boundaries) being romantic
        - I mean the moral of Grease is that completely changing yourself for your partner is completely fine. 

Note: I actually love how Grease handles it, with Danny fully prepared to go through with it, and Sandy just beating him to it. It’s also framed as her embracing who she wants to be and not strictly changing herself solely for him. But yeah it’s kinda odd as a result. Totally not the bit about that movies that’s aged the worst. 
          - FYI Grease is deliberately making fun of 1950s moralist slop movies where the good girl manages to “fix” the bad boy greaser type. The whole movie is actually a big middle finger to these types of movies. Notice how the pregnancy scare subplot is just dropped at the end? The joke is she got an abortion (pregnancy scare was a common plot in these but it ends with them getting married). The lyrics to all the songs are straight raunchy if you listen to them again. Sandie becoming a leather clad greaser girl is the whole joke. In those old movies the jocks were the straitaced good people and the leather greasers were the nihilistic degens to be “saved.” Grease also flips that completely. 

The problem is almost none of the “moralist 50s slop” movie genre survived past its era or made long term cultural impact because the “swinging 60s” immediately followed. Grease was first a musical and then adapted as a movie even further from its era. And then remained popular well to today. But it’s increasingly satirizing something nobody knows or understands anymore.
        - Blade Runner is my favorite movie, but it's always so weird when Deckard shakes Rachel until she loves him
  - I went to this movie with a gal I had been dating and I was like "this is fucked" and she said "it's romantic" and I knew it wouldn't work.
    - Wait, which of the two movies. One makes her weird and one makes her insane.
2. I’ve never seen the Notebook but I had heard all about it, that it’s this super romantic novel turned into a movie etc.

Then I saw the clip at the fair in which Gosling’s character forces McAdams’ character to go on a date with him by jumping on the Ferris wheel cart that she was in WITH HER DATE and threatening to kill himself if she refuses. It was an immediate turnoff and I lost all desire to watch the rest of the film.

## 2. Are there more examples like Robert Pattinson and Taylor Lautner, where two actors rose to global fame through the same movie or franchise but ended up with completely different career trajectories?

**Score:** 594

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Bill and Ted
  - ![gif](giphy|owi3XqnUYgQ4U)
2. You mean like.. when only one of them has a career?
  - One of their trajectories went directly into obscurity
    - Hey! He’s also famous for marrying Taylor Lautner who is currently pregnant with what I have to assume is a baby they’ll also name Taylor Lautner. 
3. Here’s a curveball I’m sure no one is thinking of. Elijah Wood and Macaulay Culkin were both in the horror film The Good Son in 1993. Culkin had already rose to prominence from roles in Uncle Buck, Home Alone, My Girl, and Home Alone 2. He was probably the most beloved child actor at that time. He took a break from acting after Richie Rich (‘94) and it wasn’t until the early 2000’s his career officially went off the rails. Conversely, Woods career was still in its infancy when The Good Son came out and he was gaining some notoriety from prior roles. Wood would go on to have a way more successful career with films like Deep Impact, The Faculty, Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind, and of course the Lord of the Rings trilogy. He’s still having great success today on tv and in film, which is awesome to see. Interesting career arcs!
4. I think these count?

Victoria Justice and Ariana Grande (Victorious) 

Tyler Posey and Dylan o’Brien (Teen Wolf)
5. Zendaya and Bella Throne did that Disney show back in the days, and were pretty big at the time. They are now on quite different places
6. Daniel Radcliffe made some weird but good/cool movies and Rupert just went on to drive around in an ice cream truck. Does that count?
  - Rupert has produced and done a few roles. Apparently he owns a lot of property 🤷‍♀️ Emma Watson I believe formally retired from acting

## 3. Who is the great actor/actress you think often overacts?

**Score:** 365
**Body:** &#x200B;

And choose a specific scene for the actor/actress you select.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Al "GRRREAT ASSS!!!" Pacino
  - ![gif](giphy|xOD2M0aHX5hjIbUbq9)
2. ![gif](giphy|j9MsVWXAFMkPcNHKxb)  
Face Off church scene. It works specifically because it’s Nicholas cage and hilarious. 
  - ![gif](giphy|14nMfF6eUeWayc)  
I see your Face/Off and raise you a Vampire’s Kiss. 
  - Nicolas Cage is such an excellent example of an actor who successfully bucks the naturalistic acting trend that so many modern actors follow, especially dramatic actors. It’s fine to be big. Be kabuki. It rules. I’m glad not everyone does it, but I’m sad that we tend to only award and praise actors give grounded performances.
3. ![gif](giphy|R9yLfikwYAF32)
4. This is a funny one because Gary Oldman said he had to do this scene a ton of times and he did it normal every time. Out of frustration and running out of different ways to say it, he did a ridiculous take and they used that one.
5. ![gif](giphy|3o751ZGnnh1aRKf34Q)
6. Gary Oldman played that part perfectly. 
7. https://preview.redd.it/a28dxhsumuah1.jpeg?width=1072&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=1bdb09f7d12031183d0d65d75654b1b213f59470

## 4. Who is The Greatest Author of Their Own Downfall in Cinematic History? (Jeff Goldblum (L) as Seth Brundle in The Fly) 🪰

**Score:** 118
**Body:** Jeff Goldblum’s Seth Brundle from David Cronenberg’s 1986 “remake” of The Fly introduces himself immediately as someone so smart, he has a device that is going to change the world… and then proceeds to suffer from some of the most baffling decision-making errors in movie history.

Let me be clear, I adore the film, and the screenwriters put enough effort into the story and Seth’s character to, at least, make us understand and empathize with some of his… let’s say, flawed problem solving skills. It has practical effects that hold up incredibly well and still ranks as a benchmark in body horror.

I just can’t help but chuckle at all the red flags that go unnoticed, the simple security features (mosquito net?) that could have been installed, the safety protocols that are overlooked. It almost makes Seth’s downfall a bit of poetic justice. I mean, this is just good advice for anyone thinking of making a major, life-changing decision while under the influence of alcohol: For God’s Sake, man, Sleep on it!!!

Is there any character who dug their own grave as deeply and efficiently as poor Seth Brundle? I look forward to your picks.

🪰

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. That guy that filled a whole island with dinosaurs. You know - for the kids.
  - https://preview.redd.it/1wvngr47lwah1.jpeg?width=588&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=7d4711adac4b46c8a761f1faa03104b794a64bd8
  - Billy and the Cloneasaurus?
2. Rorschach figures out a conspiracy to destroy NY by the smartest man in the world and tells the guy who the plot is against, and gets disintegrated for it.
3. In Zombieland they choose to pull a zombie prank on a notoriously twitchy survivor. A legend dies before his time.

![gif](giphy|Ihm7nlJmUL3xPX5fwZ)
4. Most protagonists in safdie movies.

Get in a problem because of a bad decision, decide to make worst and worst decisions to solve the original problem
5. I objectively think that the movie would have worked better with a capybara or a racoon. 
  - There are marketing problems with “The Coon”
6. Santino choosing to bring John Wick back into the game seems like a pretty big “I dun fucked up.”  Like he tried so hard, literally telling him “don’t do this.”

![gif](giphy|x10hlL4FfRzoL7YSwT)
7. ![gif](giphy|gKTrvC3x0WSHjEcZ0n)

## 5. Young Washington (2026) The most outrageous use of Gen-AI to date

**Score:** 92
**Body:** It's my first 4th July in America, so I thought I'd get into the festivities early by catching Young Washington, but now I have a severe case of humbug. The story, acting, and just about everything else is slightly below average, but my goodness whoever is in charge of the generative AI for the action has had a nightmare. From literally the opening battle scene it's so blatantly apparent that any sort of immersion is completely lost. We all know it's going to become a routine thing in movies, but surely it has to be done better than this. Anyone else seen it yet?

Full review for those interested [https://adamreviewsfilm.com/young-washington/](https://adamreviewsfilm.com/young-washington/)

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 1 if by land, 2 if by sea… 3 if by AI
  - there's gotta be a Futurama joke here somewhere
2. Watch John Adams instead.  Now there is a founding father to be proud of.  The book is fantastic as well
  - The guy who played Stannis is the same actor who played Thomas Jefferson. I wonder if there are any other Thrones/Adams actors…

“Any man who does not ratify my declaration of independence is my foe.”
  - The Adamses are fascinating. His wifes letter to him while he is at the Continental Congress are so important to show that colonial women had a bigger hand in the founding of the country than we give credit for. Abigail Adams is a bamf. She was wicked smart and advocated for women's education and the abolition of slavery. She was so influential as first lady she was called Mrs President. John trusted her implicitly. She was an economic powerhouse whole he was away and ran an illicit goods chain in silk and other luxury goods to help keep the Adams family 
3. To get into the mood I recommend Patriot. Im sure it's 10 kinds of historically inaccurate but it'll get you hyped 
  - Is it accurate: absolutely not

Is it a good movie: hell yea it is
    - Jason Isaacs is so fucking hateable in that movie. A memorable, worthwhile villain really makes a movie like that work.
  - Image of cannon ball taking guys leg out
4. I wouldn't be caught dead watching any film from angel studios 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Backrooms was so much more than I expected

**Score:** 83
**Body:** I went to see the movie mostly out of curiosity and out of need for a distraction after a long day at work.

I was fully prepared to accept and enjoy a simple horror movie with little thinking, not a lot of depth, some cheap jump scares, relatively trite ideas (e.g., lost footage) and plot devices. I even mistakenly thought it the an adaptation of the video game, and was perfectly ok with that. I had low expectations.

Instead, I got so much out of the movie and I related to it so deeply.

The way I see it, the movie is about mental illness (and less serious psychological conditions) and it offers an extensive, substantive, satisfying development of at least a couple core ideas that are not easy to work on.

The movie is deeply relatable because we all have light psychological problems we need to solve (anxiety, loops we are unable to escape, behavioral prisons of our own making, inability to make peace with traumatic episodes of our past, etc.). In addition to that, many of us have experienced at least indirectly serious mental illness in a family member or a loved one.

The movie develops at least two themes, and in my opinion it does so well.

The first theme develops the world experienced by the person suffering from serious mental illness. They fully know that they are not well and they can't function normally. They fully know that the world as they see doesn't make sense. They struggle very much in their attempts to make sense of it. This nonsensical world makes them suffer, and very much so. They live in a reality that doesn't make sense and that frightens them.

A lot of therapy work consists of exploring this scary world that doesn't make sense to them, while living in fear and in the continuous frustration caused by this broken, malfunctioning reality.

I thought that the many qualities of the main character's obsessive fixation with descending and remaining into his broken world was relatable, and a way to empathize with those suffering from mental illness.

Others have criticized the movie for the slow rhythm. I think the slow rhythm is *necessary*. The path of therapy is frequently one of very slow progress, and frequently *no progress at all*. This is real. This is how many people suffering from mental health conditions go through their life.

The fact that this world is populated with distorted and malformed copies of people and events occurred in real life is, in my opinion, also very emblematic. The mental landscape of a person suffering from mental illness (but also less serious psychological conditions) is inhabited with incorrect representations of other people that exist in real life, and events that occurred in real life. We all live surrounded by potentially unfaithful memories of what really happened and how people in our lives are. People with mental illnesses suffer from even worse distortions of reality. From the outside, those distortions are evident... but for the person suffering, those distorted elements feel **very real**.

The sequence with the main character taking a stool from the backrooms into the regular world to prove (to their therapist? to themselves?) that they are not crazy, that the world they experience is real, is such a common, relatable experience for the therapy patient *"this is the world I'm in - it might make no sense, it might be distorted, it might be full of incorrect perceptions but it is the reality I experience... to me, it's* ***completely real****. It is so hard to talk about it with others... it's so hard to prove to them I do see things that way"* 

Another theme developed pervasively (and, in my opinion, convincingly) in the movie is the theme of self-caused inprisonment: many psychological conditions consists in people forcing themselves into habit patterns that are unnecessary, don't achieve anything useful, are detrimental to us, make us miserable, and yet we can't easily resist them. We keep ourself in a mind prison of our own making.

I am sure that the movie has additional development themes that I either missed or that I was somehow blind to, as they didn't immediately serve the two main themes I was resonating to. For example, all the elements of the narrative setup (the research institute setting, the scientist seen at the end, the men in the hazmat suits, etc.). I'm fine with the movie speaking different languages to different people.

But on the couple of themes that I latched on, I thought the movie was very original, creative and imposing.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I also liked the movie a lot, but in a completely different way. 

I didn't care that much about the psychological/mental illness/personal problems aspect here since I don't think it is a particularly fresh or interesting perspective for a horror, at this point "backrooms is trauma" might not even be wrong but it's trite. 



I heard the director talk about how backrooms relate to the whole current society and time we are living in, and this is how I saw it too. 

It didn't make me think of an individual mental illness and struggle but about how the whole reality became just nonsensical, recycled collection of things that maybe had a meaning once but are now just copies of imitations, aesthetics, terms and meanings broadly applied until they mean a little, and nothing is real or interesting or unusual.  

I actually expected to be mildly bored, because I mostly need a solid story over vibes in a movie, but I was very pulled in (I knew about, but didn't watch the youtube videos before), and the whole feel of the backrooms resonated, the familiarity was achieved. It was at the same time personal and familiar, and void of all character, which was exactly right, and created a special kind of horror. 
 
When I came out, I felt a sense that that's where I live, even before thinking anything through. 

The actual characters and plot felt very secondary to me. Obviously things had to happen and I think in a way it wasn't bad, the individual contribution to backrooms worked, the memory dimension and what we take and twist from the world around us worked (the strongest moment in that respect was to see her memory of the room get processed into backroom sublayers.) The illness and trauma bits were a bit more cliche. The overall story also wasn't that strong and I understand some criticisms, especially of the final third of the movie or so, her just running from the monster over and over, and I didn't find the characters very stong either however I don't think that is a flaw in this kind of movie at all. 

More of an unresolved approach where on one hand it wants to put a story into it, but is also aware that this story is kind of secondary and just a tool to explore what it feels like to be there. I think I would make the story more banal, less focus on characters' personal problems, no therapy. Just normal people experiencing their lives reflected in backrooms, maybe a few of them. 

Still, because the story wasn't that important to me, and didn't do anything to ruin the feeling of the backrooms, this criticism really didn't affect my appreciation for this movie. I am not very susceptible, but the feeling I had that backrooms are reality stuck with me . I think it hit something real and important that can't be told through a story without it being reductive, it just needs to take you there and it did.
  - Yes, I was also grabbed by that idea of reality becoming nonsensical through iteration and being a copy of a copy. For me the real fear it evokes is the idea of information without humans, what happens when we are not there to interpret that information and give it meaning. Makes you wonder what information and memory really are.
    - But I think we area already living in that horror. Maybe it isn't yet at its worst point, but on some level we understand that most of the reality, the culture, the things we see and experience, even people's behavior and responses are just imitations of imitations of imitations, flattened into non significance. Even people, you see someone and you can grasp the type they are tyring to be and emulate, mannerisms facial expressions that repeat across faces that you saw on TV many times before (obviously not the basic ones, but even the finer, more "individualized" ones). Art. 

For me the most depressing thing about AI isn't even AI taking over human creativity, it's that for a while now human creativity is no better, uses the same process (with exceptions of course) the output is the same shit. Like everything big and significant and new already happened, already got explored, and we are just reliving and redoing it.
      - It really feels that way, but I am very cautious about my own biases so I don't end up becoming and old man yelling at a cloud. Culture is repetition and iteration, it has always been and people has always replicated what they see in order to create new things, "Everything big and significant already happened" but it happened centuries ago if you think about it, all the big themes were already explored in ancient Greece. AI, but specifically the possibility to access all of History and all Culture and remix it one click away is the new paradigm and what is fundamentally different from other eras, but the core concept of copying and reiterating is not new.
  - >I don't think it is a particularly fresh or interesting perspective for a horror, at this point "backrooms is trauma" might not even be wrong but it's trite. 

>The illness and trauma bits were a bit more cliche. 

You can share your take away of the film without being dismissive of other people's interpretations. Clearly the movie's themes on mental illness resonated strongly with OP and many more people, myself included. 

To call them trite and cliche waves away crucial elements of the message the movie was trying to touch on. Your personal takeaway is excellent but it does not warrant the belittling of other people's impressions.

>I think I would make the story more banal, less focus on characters' personal problems, no therapy. Just normal people experiencing their lives reflected in backrooms, maybe a few of them. 

Again, it's clear that Kane really wanted to hammer home a message on what therapy is and how humans navigate daily life with this movie by making it core elements of the story. 

I personally found a lot of depth behind the two main characters. There so much subtle story telling in their performance throughout that hints as to why they act the way they do and how those elements are interwoven with what the backrooms are.

To take that away would diminish the power of this film. I would go as far as to say that the movie wouldn't even exist without the characters delving so deep into their psyches.
    - I don't believe that they belittled anyone's opinions. They  essentially agreed with the OP that the therapy/trauma interpretation can be found in the film, but they did not appreciate this element of the movie or resonate with it the way OP did. It was criticism of the movie, not of OP's interpretation or reaction to it.
2. I didn't think it was a remotely interesting take on mental health/psychology. In fact it has been done to death and the writing/characters were the weakest part of the film. But I feel the story and that aspect of it was designed to be secondary to the recursive feeling of alienation and atmosphere, and maybe a effective but simplistic commentary on where the online world/AI/social media is taking us. It was a great exercise in visual design.

I read the director wants to go to film school, so I'm looking forward to see what he can do with a little more understanding of narrative, plot, dialogue, and character.
  - When you say "it has been done to death", can you point me to what you are thinking of? I'm not disputing at all. I want to research more the genre.
3. I really loved it too, I wasn’t familiar with Parson or his video series, just knew that the trailer looked interesting and it was playing in my town so I checked it out and loved every second. It was a surreal fever dream in the best ways. I wish I knew more about the researchers but I also feel like that’s part of the dream, just passing through. Incredible debut film from Parsons and I hope his next film is something new and he doesn’t get caught up in the remake money machine 
4. well i didnt read it quite that literally but i also felt the slow pace was doin real thematic work instead of just building tension, it makes u sit with the same uncertainty and repetition as the characters

## 2. First Blood (1982)

**Score:** 8
**Body:** First Blood (1982)

Rating: 9.5/10 (EXCEPTIONAL)

Watched: July 1, 2026

"All They Had To Do Was Let Him Grab A Bite To Eat"

Fun fact: I grew up pretty near to where this was filmed, and it was an enormous deal for Hope to be featured so prominently in a movie. Even though I was only 10 at the time, that was a very cool thing to have happen in our little corner of the world.

Another fun fact (or a sad one, depending): for an action fan(atic), this was only my second time seeing First Blood, separated by about 4 decades! What a mistake, because this is one hell of a movie

Even though I saw this a year or so after it was released and remembered very little about it, one thing always stuck with me:. Brian Dennehy as Sheriff Will Teasle and his *immediate* hostility. I thought they'd pace it out, but they spend exactly 1 minute before they get down to business hassling Rambo.

But it's not just Teasle that gives Rambo a hard time. It's the entire Hope PD. I actually had to a little research on why Teasle in particular was riding Rambo so hard because he comes off as so hate-filled for what *seemed to be* no reason.

To my surprise, turns out that soldiers who survived The Korean War felt forgotten and marginalized by the coverage Vietnam Vets got. Teasle is the physical embodiment of that bitterness, and Dennehy delivers.

I just can't understand that kind of mindset, but then again, I never served myself.

Everything that happens in this movie rests solely on Teasle and the others. But if they *had* treated John Rambo with respect and courtesy, we wouldn't've gotten some pretty awesome action movies.

Stallone handles the nuances of a man back from a terrible war, suffering from PTSD and who knows what else, with surprising subtlety. It's not until later that Rambo becomes a full-blown action hero, but in this first one we see exactly *why* he became so famous. HIs performance, especially at the end, is especially powerful.

All I'm saying? Everyone should've listened to baby-faced David Caruso. If they had, everyone would've had a much better time of things.

  
All in all, I found the treatment and handling of Rambo's PTSD in a movie from the 80s a lot more enlightening and thoughtful than *most* movies that came after it. That alone makes this one great movie. Everything else just adds to it.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Great write-up. One thing that makes First Blood stand out from the sequels is how little actual violence there is — Rambo barely kills anyone, it's mostly him trying to survive and escape. That final scene where he breaks down crying in Teasle's arms is one of the most raw moments in any 80s action movie. The sequels turned him into a cartoon hero, but this one is genuinely tragic.
  - I love asking people which country the first Rambo movie is set in and who the enemy combatants are.

Very rarely do you hear "Oregon. The cops."
    - I'm from Oregon and honestly never thought about it that way until someone pointed it out. The whole "enemy combatants are the local police" twist is what makes it hit different from every other 80s action movie.
      - Yeah I mean… it’s absolutely crazy that one of American media’s most cliche representations of militarism and jingoism started out as a thoughtful exploration of PTSD and the plight of veterans as told via a former green beret blowing up a town and engaging in a war with local police because they didn’t want him to eat a sandwich because he looked like a hippie.

## 3. Raging Bull (1980): From Blindness to Sight, What the Bible Quote Really Means. A Religious Analysis

**Score:** 6
**Body:** *Raging Bull* (1980) ends on a wonderful note, with this text quoted from The Bible:

>***"Speak the truth before God, we know this fellow is a sinner"***   
and the man replied: ***"Whether or not he is a sinner, I don't know, but once I was blind, Now I can see"***   
 ***-*** John 9:24-26

In the Part I of this post, let's try to explore what this quote could mean. In the Part II, we will look at *Raging Bull* from a more religious lens and analyze what makes Jake LaMotta a "sinner" in the religious sense. 

**PART I**

In the Biblical context, there was a blind man who was miraculously cured of his blindness by Jesus' touch. There were plenty sceptics of Jesus' power around that time and they approached the cured man, asking him to tell the truth and testify against Jesus by labelling him as a sinner in front of God. The cured man however, simply replies that he doesn't know if Jesus is a sinner or not but atleast he opened up his eyes.

Coming to how this quote might connect to this film, whether the things Jake LaMotta did are morally correct is a different conversation but the raw portrayal of his downfall in this film we all spent a couple hours watching atleast opens up some eyes in us. That quote nicely explains the purpose of the film without being very on the nose. The film, much like that blind man, isn't more interested in absolving or condemning Jake, it's more so interested in showing the truth of Jake's existence without flinching in an unfiltered manner. It helps us "see" how arrogance and rage corrupts a person.

I read online a couple theories from other people on how Jake's posture in his last match v/s Sugar Ray Robinson evokes the crucified posture of Jesus with the arms wide and blood pouring down the body from his wounds, which adds to this interpretation. While I don't personally rock with a person like Jake being potentially portrayed as a Christ-like figure, I can pass it off because it is subtle and the final Biblical quote also connects to this, because the person addressed in the quote is Jesus.

Or maybe this crucifixion pose is to show that Jake LaMotta has gone so far down his delusional vanity that he feels like Jesus getting crucified when he finally loses and if the whole boxing world conspired against him. He even leaves by frantically repeating *"I never went down Ray"* . Ironically enough, there is a big cross showing Christ's crucifixion placed above Jake & Vickie's bed centrally in their house, which adds weight to this theory. During the scenes before his matches, religious artifacts like crosses and images of the Virgin Mary are framed around Jake. 

Another interpretation of the quote could be the fact that Jake finally "sees". The quote is flashed on the screen right after the final scene, where Jake looks at himself in the mirror and recites *"Let's face it, It was you Charlie"* as a rehearsal for his stand up comedy. This also functions as a moment of self "reflection" into a mirror where Jake realizes the problem was always him. *"I could've been somebody, instead of a bum, which is what I am"* also cuts deep in this monologue. 

This interpretation goes along so well with confessions from the real Jake LaMotta in later parts of his life about himself, which is what made him write the autobiography in the first place to admit his wrongs. The film itself is based on that autobiography.

**PART II** 

Let's now analyze what makes Jake LaMotta a "sinner" in the religious sense. As a quick recap, Pride, Greed, Lust, Envy, Gluttony, Wrath, and Sloth are the seven deadly sins in Christianity. *Raging Bull* portrays Jake LaMotta indulge in all seven of them strongly in some way or form. 

The #1 thing I love about *Raging Bull* is the fact that it epitomizes the age old teaching and Christian saying that "Pride" is the deadliest/worst one out of the 7 deadly sins. On paper, "Pride" doesn't sound that bad right? What's so wrong in having a bit of respect for yourself and why is "Pride" said to be worse than Envy, Greed, Lust or Wrath/Rage, all of which look instantly dangerous. This film sketches out all the answers to that.

**Pride** is called the "root/father of all sins" because it doesn't just destroy you by itself but it subconsciously plants the seeds for all the 6 other deadly sins to insidiously cultivate. *"I still remember those cheers, they still ring in my ears"* is the iconic opening line of the film where Jake reflects on where his immense amounts of pride stemmed from: the success and validation being a great boxer brought him. It planted the idea in him that he is the superior and can beat down anyone in a ring and hence he can beat down anyone in his personal life, even if it is his wife or brother.

This later gives rise to 

* **Lust**, pursuing other women and staying up all night late at nightclubs while being married
* **Envy**, over Vikkie making the smallest compliment over Jake's opponent which escalated to insane amounts of paranoia over his wife
* **Wrath**, which should be self-explanatory given his nickname "Raging Bull" and during the scene where he beats up his loved ones like an animal
* **Greed**, for success and championships even if it means mutilating his opponents
* **Gluttony**, which is exemplified in the later stages of his career by Jake failing to lose weight and always eating junk food to the dismay of Joey
* and finally **Sloth**, which is all he ends up with at the end of the film, physically overweight and lazy comedian, forced into retirement due to weight struggles.

The final 20-30 minutes of this film portrays his downfall so viscerally. When Jake screams *"I'm not an animal"* all alone in an isolation prison, punching his hurtful hands against the wall, it feels like Jake has realized the biggest battle he had to win wasn't inside no boxing ring, but inside his own mental cage that he had built for himself with his vices and excessive pride.

There is this quick run-through of years in Jake LaMotta's life from 1944 to 1947 around the 40 minute mark of this film which does heavy lifting symbolically. In this segment, you get the only colour shots of the film, and those are shots of Jake "winning" in his personal life, building a family/bond with Vikkie & his brother Joey. These colour shots are intertwined with the usual Black & White shots of Jake winning games in a boxing ring.

The colour shots show something transiently beautiful that Jake never really achieves anytime in his personal life later, despite his glorious professional achievements in black & white. His hunger/rage for success in the boxing world and his tendency to get envious of his wife's normal behavior is what hinders his personal life moving forward and makes it colorless.

Overall, I think *Raging Bull* is one of the best cautionary tales that you can find in Cinema. I was even more impressed by this film on re-watch and got inspired to write this post. I still consider it to be Martin Scorsese's best movie. I wasn't surprised when I later found out that Scorsese grew up devoutly Catholic and even considered becoming a priest.

## 4. Is Godzilla Minus One Imperial Propaganda?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** [Is Godzilla Minus One Imperial Propaganda?](https://youtu.be/WahFSA7UGDM?is=bZr9l4pQ-W1qxcST)

I think Godzilla Minus One is a fantastic monster movie that finds an excellent balance between action, drama, and sincerity, however I did find myself questioning the way it depicts this time period, and Japanese denialism. 

The moment that really caught me off guard was the speech about how the imperial government didn’t value human life due citing among other things, “poorly armored tanks, and poor supply chains”. This stood out to me as an odd choice of wording because while yes these things are true, are we not going to also mention their blatant war crimes that include the butchering of hundreds of thousands of civilians & POW? The war consistently reflects on the costs that were inflected on Japanese society, but has little to nothing to say about anything beyond that, and with imperial Japan inflicting a death toll in the millions, it seems to be an odd choice. Does anyone else agree or feel the same way? 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It’s definitely narrow in that it doesn’t acknowledge the worst of its crimes, but I certainly wouldn’t describe its handling of the subject as pro-Imperial propaganda. Rather, it seems like a way to process the issue of Imperial Japan’s atrocities through the lens of intense harm done to its own people, rather than acknowledging the greater harms broadly.

And I agree that leaves a bad taste in my mouth considering how readily Japan turns a blind eye to that facet of its history, but I wouldn’t go so far as to call it propaganda for imperial Japan, so much as trying to avoid making audiences acknowledge cultural guilt (that they really should be).
  - > it seems like a way to process the issue of Imperial Japan’s atrocities through the lens of intense harm done to its own people

Which is all Japan ever does in that regard 
2. How did you feel about the fact that the movie "Saving Private Ryan" didn't address the Japanese internment camps all over the USA? Yeah, America did some shitty things along with the good things in the war, but that's not what this film is about.

For me, "Godzilla Minus One" isn't even directly about the war or imperialism or even Godzilla. It was about survivor's guilt, and showed it beautifully.
  - I think that’s a bit of a false equivalence. Not trying to downplay the Japanese internment camps by any means, but if you have a sweeping monologue about how little the government values human life, but only address that they didn’t property take care of their armies and soldiers when they were butchering hundreds of thousands of civilians is a bit strange to me.
    - The filmmaker probably wanted to be able to see his film in a Japanese theater. Too much dirty laundry makes people boycott your film. And in this case, that dirty laundry would not have helped the story in any way.
      - Godzilla, Mothra and King Ghidorah: Giant Monsters All-Out Attack did booming box office business directly criticizing Japanese militarism during the war.  

And there are certainly Japanese critics who are upset about the militaristic nostalgia of this flick.

And if you can’t picture this film being helped by, say, simply having a significant part of heroism being done by someone other than veterans who implemented a “kill all, burn all, loot all” policy your imagination seems rather limited.  Heck, they could have at least kicked “one bad apple” to the curb, and risen to the level of basic copaganda.
3. I don't think Minus One is propaganda, the propaganda is in the textbooks the kids use. Then the kids grow up and make Godzilla Minus One. Potato/Poh-tah-toh, I guess.

Since the end of the occupation, Japan has been governed mainly by right wing conservatives, and the education system they set up largely glosses over Japan's atrocities. The view that Minus Zero espouses is, as I discover time and again, pretty common. Japanese WW2 narratives run along two lines: the Japanese are victims of the Imperial government or they are victims of America or both. The first of these comes down to using Tojo as a scapegoat. There isn't much reflection of the real history because people don't know what their ancestors did.

Basically, Japan's education system is doing the equivalent of southern revisionism and getting away with it. 

The only Japanese movie I know that so much as suggests that anybody other than the Japanese suffered during WW2 is another Godzilla movie, *Godzilla, Mothra and King Ghidorah: Giant Monsters All-Out Attack*. If you know Japanese movies that acknowledge this history please let me know, I'd love to check them out. 

I do note how defensive people are being in this thread. You can love your Godzilla while also acknowledging where it's coming from.
4. I think generally speaking, countries that commit atrocities don't like talking about it later. Japan has a much cleaner reputation in modern society than it did during and after the war, so I don't know why they'd want to highlight how awful they were in whats supposed to be a fairly fun monster movie.
